                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & PEP 8
  1.2  Project Structure
  1.3  Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
  1.4  Type Hints & Static Analysis
  1.5  Error Handling
  1.6  Logging
  1.7  Documentation
  1.8  Performance & Profiling
  1.9  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Most Popular Third-Party)
  2.4  nose2 (Successor to nose)
  2.5  doctest (Tests in Docstrings)
  2.6  Framework Comparison Table
  2.7  Testing Best Practices

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & PEP 8
----------------------
- Follow PEP 8, the official Python style guide.
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Use snake_case for functions, methods, and variables.
- Use PascalCase (CapWords) for class names.
- Use UPPER_CASE for constants.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Use single quotes or double quotes consistently (pick one).
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Use absolute imports over relative imports when possible.
- Avoid wildcard imports (from module import *).

Tools to enforce style:
  - flake8  : Linter combining PyFlakes, pycodestyle, and McCabe.
  - black   : Opinionated auto-formatter.
  - isort   : Automatically sorts imports.
  - ruff    : Fast Python linter and formatter (written in Rust).

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & build config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    │   └── index.md
    └── scripts/

Key principles:
  - Use a src/ layout to prevent accidental imports of the package under
    development.
  - Keep tests/ separate from source code.
  - Use pyproject.toml for build configuration (PEP 517/518).
  - Include a README.md with installation and usage instructions.

1.3 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
-------------------------------------------------
Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies:

  python -m venv .venv          # Create a virtual environment
  source .venv/bin/activate     # Activate (Linux/macOS)
  .venv\Scripts\activate        # Activate (Windows)

Modern dependency management tools:
  - pip + requirements.txt      : Classic approach.
  - pip-tools (pip-compile)     : Generates pinned requirements from .in files.
  - Poetry                      : All-in-one dependency & packaging manager.
  - PDM                         : PEP 582-compliant, no virtualenv needed.
  - uv                          : Fast Rust-based pip/venv alternative.

Best practices:
  - Pin exact versions in production (requirements.txt with ==).
  - Use separate requirements files: requirements-dev.txt, requirements-prod.txt.
  - Regularly audit dependencies for vulnerabilities (pip-audit, safety).

1.4 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
Type hints (PEP 484) improve code clarity and enable static checking:

    def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str:
        return f"Hello {name},

  - Keep tests/ separate from source code.
  - Use pyproject.toml for build configuration (PEP 517/518).
  - Include a README.md with installation and usage instructions.

1.3 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
-------------------------------------------------
Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies:

  python -m venv .venv          # Create a virtual environment
  source .venv/bin/activate     # Activate (Linux/macOS)
  .venv\Scripts\activate        # Activate (Windows)

Modern dependency management tools:
  - pip + requirements.txt      : Classic approach.
  - pip-tools (pip-compile)     : Generates pinned requirements from .in files.
  - Poetry                      : All-in-one dependency & packaging manager.
  - PDM                         : PEP 582-compliant, no virtualenv needed.
  - uv                          : Fast Rust-based pip/venv alternative.

Best practices:
  - Pin exact versions in production (requirements.txt with ==).
  - Use separate requirements files: requirements-dev.txt, requirements-prod.txt.
  - Regularly audit dependencies for vulnerabilities (pip-audit, safety).

1.4 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
Type hints (PEP 484) improve code clarity and enable static checking:

        return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
